fr.elements: Main Building; Driveway; Landscaped Area; Pedestrian Walkways; Perimeter Fencing
fr.considerations: Single Access Point; Limited Side Access
fr.recommendations: To enhance emergency response effectiveness on the property, ensuring the driveway remains unobstructed and clearly marked could improve responder access and facilitate swift arrival to the main building. Additionally, maintaining wider, clear pedestrian walkways may assist emergency personnel by providing alternative paths for equipment deployment or personnel movement. The presence of perimeter fencing makes it important to have clearly marked and accessible entry points to expedite responder access. Since there is limited side access, ensuring that the single access point is well-illuminated and clearly visible from the main roadway could enhance navigation for emergency units, supporting efficient ingress and egress. Careful design of landscaped areas to prevent obstruction of sightlines and access points would further support effective emergency staging and operations.

wildfire.conditions: Scattered Trees; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Dense Tree Canopy; Limited Defensible Space; Vegetation Adjacent to Fence; Open Terrain
wildfire.recommendations: Improving separation between scattered trees and the structure could enhance defensible space and aid in reducing potential structure exposure to wildfire. Managing vegetation in close proximity to the structure and along the perimeter of any fencing could help decrease opportunities for flames to spread directly to these improvements. The presence of a dense tree canopy suggests the need for strategic thinning to reduce fuel continuity and enhance the defensible space around the property. Utilizing the open terrain, it may be beneficial to preserve these spaces as natural fuel breaks that can act as barriers to wildfire spread. Enhancing and maintaining these open areas could support suppression efforts and improve long-term wildfire resilience.
